title: ICO Guidance: Employment Practices and Data Protection
chapters: ICO regulatory guidance · UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018 · Covers recruitment, employment records, monitoring at work, information about workers' health, termination, and subject access requests
desc: The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) publishes guidance on data protection in the employment context, covering the entire employment lifecycle from recruitment through to post-termination records retention. The guidance explains how the UK General Data Protection Regulation (UK GDPR, retained from EU GDPR via the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018) and the Data Protection Act 2018 apply to the processing of employee personal data. Key areas include lawful bases for processing (primarily Article 6(1)(b) contract performance, Article 6(1)(c) legal obligation, and Article 6(1)(f) legitimate interests), special category data processing under Article 9 (health data, trade union membership, biometric data), employee monitoring (including email, internet, CCTV, and GPS), Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) for high-risk processing, and employees' rights including subject access requests (SARs) under Article 15. The ICO can issue enforcement notices, monetary penalty notices (fines up to £17.5 million or 4% of annual worldwide turnover), and criminal proceedings for certain offences under the DPA 2018. For Ailane, ICO Reg. No. 00013389720 is the platform's own ICO registration.
legalStatus: ICO guidance is authoritative and reflects the ICO's interpretation of the UK GDPR and DPA 2018. While not legally binding, compliance with ICO guidance is strong evidence of compliance with the legislation. Departure from ICO guidance without justification may be viewed unfavourably by the ICO and by courts.

parts.title: Lawful Bases for Processing Employee Data

sections.title: Lawful bases, consent, and legitimate interests in the employment context
sections.summary: Employers must identify a lawful basis under UK GDPR Article 6 for every processing activity involving employee personal data. The three most relevant bases in employment are: Article 6(1)(b) contract performance (processing necessary for performing the employment contract, e.g. payroll, contractual benefits, work allocation); Article 6(1)(c) legal obligation (processing required by law, e.g. PAYE, national insurance, right to work checks, statutory reporting to HMRC, HSE, or pension regulators); and Article 6(1)(f) legitimate interests (processing where the employer has a legitimate interest that is not overridden by the employee's rights and freedoms, e.g. performance management, workforce planning, internal investigations). Consent (Article 6(1)(a)) is rarely appropriate in the employment context because the power imbalance between employer and employee means consent is unlikely to be freely given. The ICO has stated that employers should avoid relying on consent as a lawful basis wherever possible and should instead identify one of the other bases. Where special category data is processed (e.g. health data for occupational health referrals, trade union membership for recognition purposes, biometric data for access control), the employer must identify both a lawful basis under Article 6 and a condition under Article 9(2) and Schedule 1 of the DPA 2018.
sections.text:
The UK GDPR requires a lawful basis for processing personal data. In the employment context, the most relevant lawful bases are:

Article 6(1)(b) — Contract performance:
• processing payroll and salary payments
• administering contractual benefits (pension, private medical insurance, company car)
• managing work allocation, shift patterns, and rotas
• providing contractual training

Article 6(1)(c) — Legal obligation:
• PAYE and national insurance deductions and reporting to HMRC
• right to work checks under the Immigration, Asylum and Nationality Act 2006
• statutory sick pay administration
• reporting under RIDDOR 2013
• pension auto-enrolment under the Pensions Act 2008
• gender pay gap reporting under the Equality Act 2010 (Specific Duties and Public Authorities) Regulations 2017

Article 6(1)(f) — Legitimate interests:
• performance management and appraisals
• workforce planning and organisational restructuring
• internal investigations (disciplinary, grievance, whistleblowing)
• monitoring for security, regulatory compliance, and quality assurance

Consent (Article 6(1)(a)) is generally NOT appropriate in employment because:
• the power imbalance means consent may not be freely given
• employees may feel unable to refuse or withdraw consent without adverse consequences
• consent must be specific, informed, and unambiguous — blanket consent provisions in employment contracts are unlikely to be valid

versions.description: UK GDPR came into force (via the EU GDPR, subsequently retained by the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018 as the 'UK GDPR'). Replaced the Data Protection Act 1998. Introduced accountability principle, DPIAs, and significantly increased maximum penalties.
Data Protection Act 2018 supplemented the UK GDPR with UK-specific provisions including Schedule 1 conditions for processing special category data and criminal offence data in the employment context.

leadingCases.name: Barhuysen v Hamilton [2016] UKUT 0290
leadingCases.heldText: The Upper Tribunal considered the lawful basis for processing employee data and confirmed that contract performance is the primary basis for most employment processing. The tribunal emphasised that employers should document their lawful basis for each category of processing.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed the primacy of contract performance as a lawful basis in employment data processing

refs: UK GDPR Art.6 (lawful bases for processing)
UK GDPR Art.9 (processing of special categories of personal data)
DPA 2018 Schedule 1 (conditions for processing special category and criminal offence data)
UK GDPR Art.13–14 (information to be provided to data subjects)
ICO Employment Practices Code (legacy — issued under DPA 1998, still referenced for practical guidance)
https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/uk-gdpr-guidance-and-resources/lawful-basis/
https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/uk-gdpr-guidance-and-resources/employment-information/

sections.title: Monitoring at work: email, internet, CCTV, GPS, and DPIAs
sections.summary: Employers who monitor employees must comply with the UK GDPR and DPA 2018. The ICO's guidance on monitoring at work covers email and internet monitoring, CCTV and video surveillance, GPS and vehicle tracking, telephone call recording, and keystroke logging and screen monitoring. Employers must have a lawful basis for monitoring (typically legitimate interests under Article 6(1)(f), requiring a Legitimate Interests Assessment), must be transparent about monitoring (employees must be told what monitoring takes place, why, and how the data is used), and must carry out a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) under Article 35 where monitoring is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals. Covert monitoring is only justified in exceptional circumstances (e.g. detection of criminal activity) and should be authorised at senior management level, time-limited, and targeted. The Investigatory Powers Act 2016 and the Regulation of Investigatory Powers Act 2000 may also apply to interception of communications. The Telecommunications (Lawful Business Practice) (Interception of Communications) Regulations 2000 permit certain types of monitoring without consent for legitimate business purposes.
sections.text:
Employers must follow these principles when monitoring employees:

• Lawful basis: identify a lawful basis under UK GDPR Article 6 (typically legitimate interests) and, for special category data, a condition under Article 9
• Transparency: employees must be informed about monitoring through a clear privacy notice and monitoring policy
• Proportionality: monitoring must be proportionate to the aim pursued; blanket or excessive monitoring is unlikely to be justified
• DPIA: a Data Protection Impact Assessment is required under Article 35 where monitoring involves systematic monitoring of a publicly accessible area, or where it is likely to result in a high risk to rights and freedoms
• Covert monitoring: only in exceptional circumstances; must be authorised at senior level; time-limited and targeted; must cease once evidence is gathered or the investigation concludes

Types of monitoring and key considerations:
• Email and internet: employers may monitor business email accounts but must balance business need against employee privacy expectations. Personal emails should not be routinely read.
• CCTV: must be registered with the ICO (if required); signage must be displayed; footage retention should be limited (typically 30 days); body-worn cameras require specific DPIAs
• GPS/vehicle tracking: justified for fleet management, lone worker safety, and insurance purposes; employees must be informed; tracking outside working hours requires strong justification
• Telephone recording: permitted for quality monitoring, training, and regulatory compliance under the Lawful Business Practice Regulations 2000; employees must be informed

versions.description: ICO published updated employment practices guidance including specific sections on monitoring at work, replacing the legacy Employment Practices Code from 2011.

leadingCases.name: Barbulescu v Romania [2017] ECHR 742 (Grand Chamber)
leadingCases.court: European Court of Human Rights (Grand Chamber)
leadingCases.heldText: The Grand Chamber held that domestic courts must balance the employer's legitimate interest in monitoring against the employee's right to respect for private life under Article 8 ECHR. Courts must consider whether the employee was notified of monitoring, the extent of the monitoring, whether the employer had legitimate reasons, whether less intrusive methods were available, the consequences for the employee, and whether adequate safeguards were in place.
leadingCases.significance: Grand Chamber authority establishing the six-factor balancing test for employee monitoring under Article 8 ECHR — directly applicable to UK law via the Human Rights Act 1998

leadingCases.name: Copland v United Kingdom [2007] ECHR 253
leadingCases.heldText: The ECHR held that monitoring of an employee's telephone calls, email, and internet usage at work constituted an interference with the right to private life under Article 8. The interference must be in accordance with the law and necessary in a democratic society.
leadingCases.significance: Established that employee monitoring engages Article 8 rights even in the workplace

refs: UK GDPR Art.6(1)(f) (legitimate interests — primary basis for monitoring)
UK GDPR Art.35 (Data Protection Impact Assessment)
UK GDPR Art.5(1)(c) (data minimisation principle)
Human Rights Act 1998 s.6 (public authorities must not act incompatibly with ECHR) / Art.8 ECHR (right to respect for private life)
Investigatory Powers Act 2016 (interception of communications)
Telecommunications (Lawful Business Practice) (Interception of Communications) Regulations 2000 (SI 2000/2699)
https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/uk-gdpr-guidance-and-resources/employment-information/

sections.title: Subject access requests: employee rights and employer obligations
sections.summary: Employees (and former employees) have the right under UK GDPR Article 15 to obtain from their employer confirmation of whether personal data concerning them is being processed, access to the personal data, and supplementary information (purposes of processing, categories of data, recipients, retention period, rights to rectification/erasure/restriction, right to lodge a complaint with the ICO, and the source of the data). The employer must respond within one calendar month of receiving the request (extendable by up to two further months for complex or numerous requests). The response must be free of charge (unless the request is manifestly unfounded or excessive). Employers must search all relevant systems including email, HR systems, personnel files, occupational health records, investigation files, CCTV footage, and managers' notes. Employers may redact data relating to other identifiable individuals (third-party data) unless those individuals consent or it is reasonable to disclose without consent. The employer must not destroy or alter data after receiving a SAR — doing so may constitute a criminal offence under DPA 2018 s.173.
sections.text:
Under UK GDPR Article 15, employees have the right to:

• confirmation of whether their personal data is being processed
• a copy of their personal data
• information about the purposes of processing, categories of data, recipients, retention periods, and their data protection rights

Employer obligations:
• respond within 1 calendar month (extendable by 2 months for complex/numerous requests)
• provide the response free of charge (a reasonable fee may be charged for manifestly unfounded or excessive requests, or the employer may refuse to act)
• search all relevant systems: HR systems, email accounts, personnel files, investigation files, management notes, occupational health records, CCTV footage, access logs, IT system logs
• redact third-party data where disclosure would be unfair to the third party, unless the third party consents or it is reasonable to disclose
• do NOT destroy or alter data after receiving a SAR — this may be a criminal offence under DPA 2018 s.173 (alteration of personal data to prevent disclosure)

Practical considerations:
• SARs during disciplinary or grievance proceedings are common — the SAR obligation is separate from and in addition to the duty to provide evidence in the disciplinary process
• SARs from former employees are valid and must be complied with in respect of data still held
• SARs cannot be refused because they are inconvenient or because the employee is in dispute with the employer
• legal professional privilege and management forecasting exemptions may apply to some documents (DPA 2018 Schedule 2 Part 5)

versions.description: UK GDPR Article 15 replaced the subject access provisions under DPA 1998 s.7. Removed the £10 fee. Reduced response time from 40 calendar days to 1 calendar month. Broadened the information that must be provided.

leadingCases.name: Dawson-Damer v Taylor Wessing LLP [2017] EWCA Civ 74
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal considered the scope of a subject access request and held that the data controller must make a reasonable and proportionate search for the data requested. The data controller is not required to search every system exhaustively but must make reasonable efforts to locate the data.
leadingCases.significance: Established the proportionate search standard for subject access requests

leadingCases.name: Ittihadieh v 5-11 Cheyne Gardens RTM Company Ltd [2017] EWCA Civ 121
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the right of subject access applies to personal data contained within correspondence, including email. The data controller must search email accounts and produce personal data contained in emails, subject to exemptions and proportionality.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed that email correspondence falls within the scope of subject access requests

refs: UK GDPR Art.15 (right of access by the data subject)
UK GDPR Art.12 (transparent information, communication and modalities)
DPA 2018 s.173 (alteration of personal data to prevent disclosure — criminal offence)
DPA 2018 Schedule 2 Part 5 (exemptions: legal professional privilege, management forecasts, negotiations)
ICO Subject Access Request Code of Practice
https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/uk-gdpr-guidance-and-resources/individual-rights/right-of-access/

pendingAmendments: UK GDPR retained post-Brexit — ICO continues enforcement
AI Act implications for automated employment decision-making — monitoring
ERA 2025 — no direct amendments to data protection but Fair Work Agency information-gathering powers interact

parliamentaryOrigin.statutoryBasis: ICO guidance issued under UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018. Authoritative interpretation of data protection obligations as they apply to the employment relationship.
parliamentaryOrigin.legalEffect: Not legally binding but treated as authoritative by the ICO in enforcement actions and by tribunals. Departure without justification may be viewed unfavourably.
parliamentaryOrigin.note: The ICO employment data protection guidance covers: lawful basis for processing employee data, privacy notices for employees, subject access requests from employees, data retention, employee monitoring (cross-reference ico-mon.json), recruitment data, health data, criminal records data, and international transfers of employee data.

precedingDocuments.title: UK GDPR (Retained EU Law)
precedingDocuments.significance: The Data Protection Act 2018 and the UK GDPR (retained EU law) establish the data protection framework that applies to employment data.

precedingDocuments.title: ICO Employment Practices Code (2011, legacy)
precedingDocuments.significance: The predecessor guidance issued under the Data Protection Act 1998. Replaced by the current UK GDPR-aligned guidance.

leadingCases.name: Nowak v Data Protection Commissioner [2017] CJEU Case C-434/16
leadingCases.court: Court of Justice of the EU
leadingCases.heldText: Examination scripts and examiners' comments about a candidate constitute personal data. The concept of personal data is broad and includes any information relating to an identified or identifiable individual.
leadingCases.significance: Broad interpretation of personal data — includes employment records, performance assessments, and notes about employees.

leadingCases.name: Ittihadieh v 5-11 Cheyne Gardens RTM Co Ltd [2017] EWCA Civ 121
leadingCases.heldText: Subject access requests can cover emails and documents mentioning the data subject by name. The scope of a SAR is broad and employers must search all relevant systems.
leadingCases.significance: Scope of subject access requests — employers must search broadly.

leadingCases.name: Barbulescu v Romania [2017] ECHR 742
leadingCases.heldText: Six-factor balancing test for employee monitoring under Article 8.
leadingCases.significance: Definitive balancing test for monitoring — applies across all employment data processing.
